how_do_you_identify_yourself.other.answer: I am also a legal guardian and primary advocate for my teenage son, who receives home- and community-based services through Vermont’s developmental services system. I participate actively in his Individualized Education Program (IEP) team at school and his Individualized Service Plan (ISP) through his Designated Agency. I also facilitate a small informal support group for other parents of youth with developmental disabilities in Washington County.

how_do_you_identify_yourself.i_describe_.answer: My 15-year-old son, Ethan, has an autism spectrum disorder with an accompanying intellectual disability and significant sensory processing challenges. He was diagnosed shortly before his third birthday after we noticed delayed speech, limited eye contact, and repetitive behaviors like hand-flapping and lining up toys. Over the years, his disability has affected his communication, learning pace, and ability to manage transitions and unexpected changes in routine.

Ethan is mostly non-speaking and uses a combination of a speech-generating device, picture exchange communication, and some American Sign Language signs to communicate his needs and preferences. He also has co-occurring anxiety, which can intensify when environments are loud, crowded, or unpredictable. Despite these challenges, he is very social in his own way, loves schedules and calendars, has a strong visual memory, and thrives when adults and peers take the time to communicate with him in ways that work for him. His disability is lifelong and impacts his daily functioning, but with appropriate supports, he is increasingly able to make choices about his own life and participate in our community.
